data.title: UU Wallet Secures U.S. MSB License, Embarking on a New Chapter of Global Compliance with Security at Its Core
data.description:
Hong Kong, March 13, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — UU Wallet, a leading global Web3 payment platform, recently announced that it has officially obtained the Money Services Business (MSB) license from the United States. This makes UU Wallet one of the few digital financial service platforms that combines robust security capabilities with global compliance credentials. The approval of this license signifies that UU Wallet can now operate compliantly within the U.S. regulatory framework, engaging in digital asset transactions, fiat currency exchange, and cross-border payments, further solidifying its position as the "most secure Web3 payment platform."

Security and Compliance: Driving Global Strategy  

The U.S. MSB license, issued by the Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN) under the Department of the Treasury, is a critical entry requirement for international financial institutions to provide monetary services. Passing the license audit indicates that UU Wallet has met stringent U.S. regulatory standards in areas such as anti-money laundering (AML), risk control systems, and user asset protection.

Since its establishment in 2023, UU Wallet has rapidly grown into a Web3 payment ecosystem covering over 160 countries and regions, thanks to its technological innovation and commitment to security. With a team of over 100 professionals, UU Wallet focuses on four core businesses: digital currency and fiat exchange, prepaid cards, enterprise wallet services, and Web3 wealth management. Its daily transaction volume has surpassed $20 million.

Four Core Features Redefining Secure Payment Experiences

Instant Fiat Exchange, Secure and Frictionless  

UU Wallet supports instant conversions between mainstream fiat currencies such as the Philippine Peso (PHP), South Korean Won (KRW), Japanese Yen (JPY), US Dollar (USD), Hong Kong Dollar (HKD), and Malaysian Ringgit (MYR), and digital currencies. Utilizing intelligent routing and multi-signature cold wallet technology, users can complete cross-border fund transfers within 5 seconds, with end-to-end data encryption and a zero-risk incident record.

Global Prepaid Cards: USDT Real-Time Circulation, Balancing KYC Efficiency and Security

Users can instantly recharge prepaid cards with USDT, which are accepted in over 160 countries and regions for both online and offline purchases. The platform supports both virtual and physical cards, as well as B2B client integration. UU Wallet's unique "tiered KYC" mechanism allows basic consumption scenarios to complete identity verification in just 3 minutes, while larger transactions are secured through dynamic facial recognition and blockchain address tracking, striking a balance between convenience and security.

Enterprise Wallet Services: Comprehensive Fund Protection

For Web3 enterprises, UU Wallet offers payment and fund management solutions covering 50+ public chains and 10,000+ tokens. Through secure multi-party computation (MPC) technology, real-time risk warning systems, and monthly third-party audits, UU Wallet ensures transparent and traceable fund flows, mitigating risks of operational errors and hacker attacks.

Web3 Asset Wealth Management: Balancing Returns and Risk Control

The platform provides a variety of value-added services, including flexible staking, structured products, and compliant stablecoin wealth management. All products are executed automatically via smart contracts, with code security verified by top-tier auditing firms. Historical annualized returns range from 3% to 15%, ensuring users' assets grow steadily and securely.

"Security is the lifeline of financial services," said a UU Wallet spokesperson. "With the MSB license, we will accelerate collaboration with global regulators and traditional financial institutions, driving deeper integration of Web3 payments with mainstream economic systems."

About UU Wallet

UU Wallet is a security-focused Web3 payment infrastructure provider dedicated to lowering the barriers to digital asset usage through technological innovation. The company has obtained financial licenses in the United States and the Philippines, serving over 500,000 individual users and 2,000 enterprises with a zero-security-incident record.

Wesley Tam

pamela@uuwallet.com

https://www.uuwallet.com/

data.title: Alfa Financial Software Posts Higher FY24 Attributable Profit, Revenue
data.description:
Alfa Financial Software Holdings said Thursday its profit and revenue for 2024 rose year over year.

Profit attributable to equity holders for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was 25.6 million pounds sterling, compared with 23.5 million pounds earlier. EPS moved to 0.0856 pound from 0.079 pound.

The financial software company's revenue was 109.9 million pounds, compared with 102 million pounds a year ago.

Meanwhile, the board proposed a final dividend of 0.014 pound per share and a special dividend of 0.024 pound per share. The final dividend is payable June 27 to shareholders on record May 30, and the special dividend is payable May 30 with a record date of May 2. It paid a final dividend of 0.013 pound per share and a special dividend of 0.020 pound per share a year ago. This takes the full-year dividend to 0.038 pound per share, up 15% year over year.

For 2025, the company expects mid-teens growth in subscription revenue and a "strong" revenue momentum in its delivery and software engineering segment.

data.title: Trainline Kicks Off First Tranche of GBP75 Million Share Buyback Program
data.description:
Trainline launched the first 37.5 million-pound-sterling tranche of its share buyback program worth up to 75 million pounds on Thursday, according to a same-day filing.

The independent rail and coach travel platform mandated Morgan Stanley & Co. International to conduct the first tranche, while it tapped Numis Securities for the second tranche.

Trainline noted that all repurchased shares will be canceled or stored in its treasury.

data.title: Tech, Media & Telecom Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Technology, Media and Telecom. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0755 GMT - One way for China to rebuff Trump's tariff threat is to develop its own critical technologies, Natixis analysts write in a note. China has used the Two Sessions meeting to respond to Trump's policies, they say. As China has introduced additional measures to support the development of critical technologies, especially artificial intelligence, that could help the country further reduce its technological dependence on U.S., they add. DeepSeek's success in late January has instigated a recovery in stock markets in China and Hong Kong, they note. Supportive measures by Beijing could furthersustain the positive sentiment on China's tech sector, Natixis says. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0229 GMT - Naver's commission adjustments, effective June 2, are set to boost revenue and earnings, Nomura analysts Angela Hong and Won Kang write in a note. The South Korean internet platform giant's new take-rate structure of reduced commissions can lead sellers to spend more on advertising, they say. The company has room to improve its e-commerce monetization given its low rates of commissions to sales, they note. They raise their earnings-per-share estimates for Naver by 4%-15% for 2025-2026. Nomura upgrades its rating on the stock to buy from neutral and raises its target price by 18% to KRW260,000. Shares are 1.4% higher at KRW217,000. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0054 GMT - Macquarie analysts turn bearish on Australia's Audinate, citing potential headwinds including trade tariffs. Cutting their recommendation on the stock to underperform from neutral, the analysts tell clients in a note that they see no evidence for optimism on the audio-visual equipment maker. They point to softer economic growth, tariffs, weak market data, and continued discounting of adaptors to support their view. Management is responding soundly to softer demand but these market conditions are set to persist, they add. Macquarie cuts its target price 28% to A$6.30. Shares are down 0.7% at A$6.76. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2246 GMT [Dow Jones]--CFRA Research analyst Angelo Zino says in a research note that he views Lip-Bu Tan's appointment as Intel's chief executive positively, citing Tan's strong industry background and previous familiarity with the company's operations. "His immediate challenges include deciding whether to maintain (Intel's) integrated structure or pursue a break-up strategy," he writes. Ultimately, the company's success depends on whether it can develop competitive products in the AI-driven data center market, defend its PC market share and address its falling stock price, Zino says. "Dialogue with the U.S. government on foundry funding will also be critical." Shares rise 11%. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

2131 GMT - Intel appoints Lip-Bu Tan as its new chief executive, succeeding co-CEOs David Zinsner and Michelle Johnston Holthaus. The two stepped into the role in December following Pat Gelsinger's abrupt retirement. With Tan's appointment, Zinsner will return to his role of chief financial officer and executive vice president, while Johnston Holthaus will return to her role as CEO of Intel Products. Gelsinger, at the time of his departure, was in the middle of a multiyear turnaround strategy that involved spending billions of dollars building new factories and growing a contract chip-manufacturing business. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

2126 GMT - Intel's new CEO Lip-Bu Tan says he respects and admires the semiconductor company, and that he sees significant opportunities for it to remake its business to better serve customers and create shareholder value. "Intel has a powerful and differentiated computing platform, a vast customer installed base and a robust manufacturing footprint that is getting stronger by the day as we rebuild our process technology roadmap," he says. "I am eager to join the company and build upon the work the entire Intel team has been doing to position our business for the future." Shares rise 12% after hours. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

2117 GMT - Intel names Lip-Bu Tan as its new top executive, effective March 18. Tan, who previously served on the beleaguered semiconductor company's board of directors, has more than 20 years of semiconductor and software experience, having formerly held the top role at Cadence Design Systems. At Cadence, Tan helped turn around the company, focusing on what Intel said was customer-centric innovation that helped double revenue, expand operating margins and send its stock higher. His appointment comes as Intel's core business of making chips for personal computers and servers has been challenged amid an artificial-intelligence boom that has largely benefited rival Nvidia. Shares rise 11% in after-hours trading. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1548 GMT - President Trump's new tariffs on aluminum and steel will take a toll on the entire auto industry, with some of the biggest price surges expected for larger-model vehicles like pickup trucks and SUVs, Kevin Roberts of CarGurus says in a note. The scale of the price hikes will depend on each automaker's sourcing and production chain, he says, but vehicles that use more materials will see the biggest markups, Roberts says. The levies will have a more immediate impact on the prices of used cars if the cost of auto parts goes up, he says. The used market is more supply-restrained, especially given high demand for vehicles that are three years old or younger, so anyone considering those models should purchase sooner rather than later, Roberts says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1401 GMT - Transcontinental is well-positioned to navigate a tariff situation, especially with its profits largely derived from Canada. In a CIBC report, Hamir Patel says about 10% of Transcontinental's revenue could be potentially affected by tariffs, the packaging company says a 25% tariff would hit Ebitda by about C$10 million-C$15 million--which is only 2%-3% of expected Ebitda for the 2025. Patel says that unlike some of its Canadian flexible packaging peers which predominantly have assets concentrated in Canada, Transcontinental has operations in both, "which may provide it with an advantageous position if there are tariffs going in both directions." (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1133 GMT - Advanced artificial-intelligence models can be developed without spending billions of dollars as evidenced by DeepSeek, Kaan Terzioglu, chief executive of digital operator Veon, says in an interview. Veon developed an open-source large language model in Kazakhstan for less than $2.5 million in about nine months, Terzioglu says. Veon developed the model in a consortium that included research institutes coordinated by the Ministry of Digital, Innovations, and Aerospace Industry of Kazakhstan. (mauro.orru@wsj.com)

1048 GMT - Ukrainian telecommunications company Kyivstar decided to list in the U.S. as it is the market with the highest liquidity in the world, the chief executive officer of parent company Veon said. Kyivstar's listing choice reflects its understanding of the regulatory landscape and investors' expectations, CEO Kaan Terzioglu said last week during an interview at the Mobile World Congress, in Barcelona. "We believe that the liquidity, the confidence and the interest in America's stock market is far better than in other stock markets around the world," Terzioglu said. Veon Group delisted its shares from the Euronext Amsterdam exchange in favour of the Nasdaq in the fourth quarter. Kyivstar will make history as the first Ukrainian company listed in the U.S. stock exchange, Chief Executive Officer Oleksandr Komarov added.(najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

1042 GMT - Veon has committed to invest $1 billion in Ukraine over five years, Chief Executive Officer Kaan Terzioglu says in an interview. Terzioglu sees an opportunity to rebuild the infrastructure of the country, noting that the telecommunications group has been investing every day in the country. "In frontier markets, we have not invested in 5G, but Ukraine is a market where we will invest in the latest technologies in the rebuilding phase," he adds. Frontier markets are those whose economies are considered less mature than emerging markets. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0800 GMT - Assicurazioni Generali's dividend growth is solid, J.P. Morgan says in a research note after the Italian insurer posted 2024 results in line with expectations. The dividend payout, which grew 12% compared with the previous year, was stronger than expected, analysts write. Management reiterated its ambition to grow its dividend by more than 10% annually over the next three years and while consensus seems to already have factored in this for 2025, there is upside to consensus for 2026 and 2027, they add. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0756 GMT - Assicurazioni Generali posted a solid set of numbers, Morgan Stanley says in a research note after the Italian insurer's 2024 results. Its headline group operating profit was ahead of consensus expectations. "When looking through the detail, this appears to be a clean, high quality beat, particularly in [property and casualty]," analysts write. They note that a potential question mark will be the miss in life contractual service margin due to weaker economic and operating variances and while they expect this to be in focus in the call with management, they don't see it as enough to detract from the positives elsewhere in the print. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0752 GMT - Bridgepoint's better-than-expected full-year results should boost its share price following a period of weakness, JPMorgan analysts say in a note. The British private-equity firm's Ebitda margin guidance of 52%-55% in 2025/2026 compares with consensus estimates of 50% in 2025 and 53% in 2026, they say. Bridgepoint's adjusted Ebitda topped company-compiled consensus, whereas total expenses were in line with estimates, JPMorgan says. Its adjusted profit before tax missed expectations by 7% as consensus didn't properly account for the cost of additional debt used to finance the acquisition of infrastructure investor Energy Capital Partners, as well as foreign currency headwinds, they say.(helena.smolak@wsj.com)

0120 GMT - There are multiple reasons why Westpac is likely to underperform the broader Australian share market in 2025, according to the bank's bears at Morgan Stanley. They highlight eight reasons for their continued underweight rating, but point to three as the most important: margin trends, capital management and the lender's ongoing tech overhaul. The analysts point out in a note to clients that margins and capital both fell short of expectations in Westpac's first fiscal quarter, while investors are waiting on more detail about the tech overhaul benefits. Raising their expense estimates, the MS analysts cut their target price by 6.5% to A$27.30. Shares are down 1.3% at A$29.68. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

2358 GMT - New Zealand needs to remain open to diversifying its dairy exports, says Forsyth Barr, citing the slowdown in purchases by its top customer. China accounts for 35% of New Zealand's dairy exports, but local production has been rising at a time when demand is weak. Analyst Matt Montgomerie highlights that China's milk production has increased at a compound annual rate of 5% over the past five years. Its production relative to New Zealand is now at its highest level in two decades. "Rising China milk production and New Zealand's reliance on China (also 40% of Fonterra's EBIT excluding Consumer) remain key risks for the industry," Forsyth Barr says. China remains the weak spot in an otherwise bullish moment for New Zealand dairy producers, with milk prices at record highs and costs stabilizing.(david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1821 GMT - Front-month gold settles up 0.9% to $2,939.10 a troy ounce, making it the sixth session out of the past eight that front-month gold closed with a positive finish. The escalating tariff battle seen today — with the EU instituting retaliatory tariffs and Canada adding new levies — is helping propel gold, says Peter Cardillo of Spartan Capital Securities. "We believe gold is moving higher due to the prospects of the trade war intensifying in the coming weeks," he says. "The gold market is rising in response to potential inflation stemming from the trade war and uncertainties surrounding global economic activity."SPDR Gold rises 0.6%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0858 GMT - Banca Popolare di Sondrio's new plan envisages a radical change in shareholder remuneration, Equita says in a research note. The Italian lender pledged a dividend payout of 85% for the next three years. This compares to the broker's 70% ratio estimate and the company's 63% ratio for 2024, analyst Andrea Lisi writes. The new targets are broadly in line with his estimates for 2025 but higher for 2027 due to higher revenue generation and lower risk costs, he notes. The analyst adds that Equita's targets are typically more conservative than company guidance. Shares in Sondrio--which last month rebuffed a takeover approach from larger peer BPER Banca--rise 2.1% to 11.05 euros and have gained 36% year to date. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0848 GMT - An acquisition of U.K. insurer Esure by Belgium's Ageas would be a positive step if the price is right but Ageas isn't likely to follow through, ING says in a research note. Reuters reported that Ageas and German peer Allianz are among the bidders for Esure, which is owned by Bain Capital, with a price tag of at least 1 billion pounds. "Price could go higher with bidding but Ageas is unlikely to chase this, since it is a PWC geared business (Price Web Comparison), when Ageas CEO has shown more interest in broker or direct distribution channel," analyst Jason Kalamboussis writes. If the price is around the 1.3 billion-pound mark, Bain Capital will sell at a small profit on its 1.21 pound billion acquisition in 2018, the analyst says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0842 GMT - Legal & General is making strides as it enters a new chapter, Interactive Investor says in a market comment after the British group's 2024 results. "The progress of the group is almost swan-like, with gradual movements masking some furious paddling underneath the water," head of markets Richard Hunter writes, pointing to the streamlining of the business announced last June, which led to non-core disposals that will boost shareholder returns. Core operating profit rose 6% with contributions from each of its businesses, driven mainly by its institutional retirement unit, which has a busy pipeline for pension risk transfer deals. Shares trade 0.7% lower at 243 pence, paring some earlier losses, while the FTSE 100 edges up 0.2%. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0004 GMT - New Zealand investors have dropped the chance of Contact Energy securing regulatory clearance for its takeover of Manawa Energy to less than 40%, Forsyth Barr says. It believes Contact has offered a full price for Manawa, and it will take several years to deliver savings needed to justify the transaction cost. Analyst Andrew Harvey-Green doesn't expect Contact's share price to react much whatever New Zealand's Commerce Commission decides about the deal. "The situation is different for Manawa," Forsyth Barr says. "The passage of time means it is increasingly difficult to estimate the size of the market reaction to no deal." It thinks Manawa's share price could fall to around NZ$4.00 if the takeover is blocked, from NZ$4.88 currently. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1928 GMT - Oil futures gain as the EIA's report of a 1.4 million barrel increase in U.S. crude oil stocks is offset by a bigger-than-expected 5.7 million drawdown in gasoline inventories. U.S. inflation for February came in lower than expected, and OPEC kept its demand growth estimates for 2025 and 2026 unchanged at 1.45 million barrels a day and 1.43 million b/d, respectively. Oil's push toward the top of its recent $3 trading range despite stock market losses "is sending off some supportive vibes suggestive of further upside," Ritterbusch says in a note. While the drop in oil prices since President Trump took office "looks successful thus far," it has been for the wrong reasons "i.e., tariff-related expectations for global oil demand deterioration," the firm adds. WTI settles up 2.2% at $67.68 a barrel, and Brent rises 2% to $70.95 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1345 GMT - Oil futures are higher with some support from a softer-than-expected U.S. inflation reading that's keeping the dollar weaker, while the market awaits U.S. oil inventory data from the EIA. U.S. crude stocks are expected to have risen by 1.2 million barrels, according to a Wall Street Journal survey of analysts, with declines seen in gasoline and distillate stocks. OPEC left its oil demand growth forecasts for this year and next at 1.45 million barrels a day and 1.43 million b/d, respectively. The producer group said that so far trade tariffs "are not anticipated to materially impact current underlying growth assumptions," although they increase uncertainty. WTI is up 1.6% at $67.29 and Brent is up 1.4% at $70.51.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1119 GMT - Harbour Energy's cash-flow from operations in 2027 is likely to be 40% lower than in 2025 despite just a 5.5% decline in production, Berenberg analysts write. After the Wintershall Dea deal, Harbour's exposure to European gas prices is higher and the analysts expect prices to fall from $12 per million British thermal unit in 2025 to $7.5 mmbtu in 2027. This is due to an increase in supply and means that operating cash flow should to fall to $2 billion in 2027 from $3.5 billion in 2025, they add. This decline is much sharper than the 5.5% reduction in Harbour's production base that the analysts expect. Shares fall 2% to 181.85 pence and are down 32% over the past year.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0752 GMT - K+S shares are likely to trade largely flat after the company reported a set of results with both positive and negative aspects and issued a cautious outlook for 2025, analysts at Morgan Stanley say in a research note. The German fertilizer company's fourth-quarter earnings were better than consensus expectations thanks to improved agriculture volumes, but free cash flow came in weaker than anticipated, Morgan Stanley says. K+S's 2025 guidance looks cautious, according to the analysts. The mid-point of its 2025 Ebitda outlook range of between 500 million and 620 million euros is slightly above the consensus forecast, but its volume guidance disappoints largely due to demand and logistics uncertainty, Morgan Stanley says. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0715 GMT - Merdeka Copper Gold may benefit from two significant projects--TB Copper and Pani Gold, CGS International analysts say in a research report as they resume coverage of the stock with an add rating and target price of IDR2,400.00. TB Copper, a copper mine in Indonesia, is expected to begin production in 2028 and contribute around US$360 million Ebitda, the analysts estimate. Pani Gold, a gold mine in Indonesia, is expected to start production in 2026 and contribute US$91 million Ebitda, they estimate. With the start of these two projects, three-year Ebitda CAGR for the Indonesian producer of gold, copper and other minerals could reach 54%, the analysts add. Shares are 0.4% lower at IDR1,325.00. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0103 GMT - Rio Tinto's production and free cash flow are likely to accelerate faster than rival BHP's over the rest of this decade, Goldman Sachs analysts say in a note. Alongside valuation, that's why GS prefers Rio's stock over BHP, both of which it rates a buy, the analysts say. Despite both miners spending around US$10 billion-US$11 billion on capex annually, Rio's copper-equivalent production should rise about 20% by the end of the decade. BHP's output is expected to remain largely flat, with its major projects mostly longer dated, say the analysts.(rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0042 GMT - Orica's A$400 million share buyback confirms the explosives maker's near-term focus is on integrating recent acquisitions, and not pursuing more M&A, Macquarie analysts say in a note. Of Orica's integration efforts, "Terra is performing well following [the] successful Ground Probe acquisition whilst Cyanaco remains [a] work in progress," they say. The analysts were pleased to see the much-anticipated buyback eventuate, even if it was "a case of 'buy the rumor, sell the fact'" for investors, they say. After falling by 1.3% Wednesday, Orica's shares are up 1.3% in Sydney at A$16.895. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2253 GMT - Algoma Steel Group says that tariffs on steel and aluminum will have a material and adverse effect on its finances and operations. That is because tariffs have driven steel prices below production costs for much of the industry, as well as heightened general macroeconomic uncertainty. "We remain confident that the Canadian federal and provincial governments will respond swiftly and appropriately to support the industry," CEO Michael Garcia says. "Given the deeply integrated North American supply chain, we believe rational dialogue will prevail between these close allies, restoring normal steel trade." The Canadian company is currently assessing the tariffs, adding that it is too early to estimate the full extent of their effects at this time. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

2203 GMT - Alkane Resources's Boda-Kaiser project in eastern Australia has become more valuable as gold prices hover near a record high, bolstering its appeal to potential investors, says Bell Potter. A scoping study released mid last year pointed to a net present value of A$1.1 billion after tax. That was based on a gold price of A$3,500/oz. Now, however, the gold price has risen to A$4,640/oz. Analyst Bradley Watson says the spot gold price suggests a post-tax net present value of A$2.1 billion. "We think the value of Boda at current gold prices will not be lost on potential partners that are scouring the gold sector for still-undervalued opportunities," says Bell Potter, which rates Alkane a buy. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2139 GMT - Ramelius Resources will increasingly need to consider M&A, says Ord Minnett, after an updated plan for the Mt Magnet gold mine in Western Australia disappointed the market. The new plan highlights a steeper production fall at Mt Magnet in FY 2026-FY 2031, analyst Paul Kaner says. Ord Minnett had previously thought the Eridanus cut-back/mill expansion could plug some of this shortfall. Now, however, it appears that Ramelius will need M&A to address this, especially if its Rebecca-Roe development is also pushed out. "As a result, the market will likely place an M&A discount on the stock, regardless of track record given the higher pricing environment (i.e. seller's market)," Ord Minnett says. The bank downgrades Ramelius to accumulate, from buy. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1831 GMT - Labrador Iron Ore Royalty faces lower growth and a challenging tariff landscape, TD Cowen's Craig Hutchison says in a report, downgrading the stock to hold from buy. The analyst notes a 4Q earnings miss, though cash-flow per share was above TD estimates. He also mentions sputtering steel demand as a risk amid escalating tariffs and softer global growth. Shares are down 3.4% to C$29.15, and off 3.5% in the past 12 months. TD Cowen also lowers its price target to C$31 from C$32. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

1812 GMT - The U.S. imposed 25% tariffs on all steel and aluminum imports earlier today, but President Trump had threatened a 50% tariff on Canadian steel and aluminum Tuesday, before relenting. That additional tariff would likely change trade flows, but not in a way that would benefit U.S. manufacturing, Alcoa CFO Molly Beerman says at the JPMorgan Industrials Conference. Under proposed higher tariffs, Alcoa would most likely send its aluminum produced in Canada to Europe, she says. "That supply may very well make its way over to the U.S.," Beerman says, adding that to have ships crossing the Atlantic with the same product just to get the right tariff treatment would be very disruptive. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1743 GMT - Alcoa "would not make a decision on building a new smelter based on tariffs," CFO Molly Beerman says at the JPMorgan Industrials Conference. "These are decisions that you're looking out 20 to 40 years." The company could restart some of its idle U.S. capacity, she says, though she adds that those smelters have not been run for a very long time and would be very expensive to restart. "We are looking at the numbers but we'll want to see the cost to restart, as well as how the tariffs stick before we make a decision on whether we'll bring that back online," Beerman says. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1703 GMT - Alcoa has two smelters in the U.S. that produce about 290,000 metric tons of aluminum annually, both of which stand to benefit from tariffs enacted on imports of steel and aluminum, CFO Molly Beerman says at the JPMorgan Industrials Conference. "However, our Canadian operations produce three times that amount," she says. A 25% tariff on the aluminum that Alcoa produces in Canada would be a net negative for the company, resulting in the loss of tens of millions of dollars, Beerman says. The loss isn't likely to hurt the company's first quarter, but could affect future earnings, she adds. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

1654 GMT - Alcoa is working closely with President Trump's administration, as well as trade groups in the U.S. and Canada, to decipher the effect that tariffs on steel and aluminum will have on the industry, CFO Molly Beerman says at the J.P. Morgan Industrials Conference. "While we're very supportive of (the administration's) efforts to improve the industry, as well as strengthen U.S. manufacturing jobs, we do see that there could be some harm from the tariffs," she says, adding that Alcoa is particularly focused on gaining a Canadian exemption. The company is also having discussions about energy policy, because "affordable and access to energy, particularly renewable, is critical in any decision for additional aluminum investment in the long term," Beerman says. Shares rise 3.3%. (connor.hart@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0427 GMT - Cathay Pacific's earnings beat doesn't compel Citi Research to turn positive on the carrier. The reluctance comes despite Citi's view that mid- to long-haul pricing will unlikely come under significant pressure in 2025 due to tight supply of wide-body jets. Analysts led by Amy Han cite the industry's falling cargo yields on Pacific and Asia-Europe routes this year as a reason for caution. Cathay could face additional downward pressure on demand and yields if the U.S. eventually cancels the de minimis trade exemption for Chinese goods, they say, noting the airline's relatively large exposure to cross-border e-commerce, especially from China. Citi maintains its neutral rating and HK$10.50 target price on the stock. Shares are 5.7% lower at HK$10.28 midday. (farah.elias@wsj.com)

2211 GMT - Military shipbuilder Austal's A$200 million capital raising to expand its shipyard in Mobile, Alabama, doesn't scotch hopes of EPS growth, contends Bell Potter. Analyst Daniel Laing says the dilutive affect of the equity raise and changes to Austal's capex outlook drive EPS downgrades of 7% and 15% in FY 2025 and FY 2026, respectively. Still, Bell Potter says it forecasts "substantial EPS growth in FY 2025/FY 2026/FY 2027 and we believe the reiterated FY 2025 guidance of Ebit not less than A$80 million still looks conservative." It retains a buy call on Austal's stock. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1838 GMT - U.S. auto dealer views on electric vehicle sales improved in 1Q from the final quarter of 2024, but are still weak, Cox Automotive says in its latest Automotive Dealer Sentiment Index. The 1Q index indicates many dealers feel EV sales are worse now than a year ago, Cox says. Expectations for the EV market rose in 1Q, but the score of 40 shows most dealers expect the EV market will decline, rather than grow, over the next three months, according to Cox. The survey also shows dealers continue to view the EV tax credit favorably, citing its benefits for both dealerships and sales, Cox says. (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1727 GMT - Cox Automotive said its first-quarter Automotive Dealer Sentiment Index showed an upward shift in dealer sentiment, along with optimism for the spring selling season. Dealers' view of current market conditions rose from to index score of 44 in the first quarter from 42 in the fourth quarter. A score below the threshold of 50 indicates more dealers view the market as weak rather than strong, Cox said. But it added the market outlook index rose for the second consecutive quarter, reaching 58, its highest score since 2022. Cox Automotive Chief Economist Jonathan Smoke said "a theme that continues through all of the findings is that the first quarter is better than a year ago for sure." (stephen.nakrosis@wsj.com)

1548 GMT - President Trump's new tariffs on aluminum and steel will take a toll on the entire auto industry, with some of the biggest price surges expected for larger-model vehicles like pickup trucks and SUVs, Kevin Roberts of CarGurus says in a note. The scale of the price hikes will depend on each automaker's sourcing and production chain, he says, but vehicles that use more materials will see the biggest markups, Roberts says. The levies will have a more immediate impact on the prices of used cars if the cost of auto parts goes up, he says. The used market is more supply-restrained, especially given high demand for vehicles that are three years old or younger, so anyone considering those models should purchase sooner rather than later, Roberts says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1520 GMT - Deutsche Post's cost-cutting program is positive as it offers the company a pillar of EBIT growth in 2026/2027, UBS analysts write. The program seeks to make longer-term and structural adjustments to the cost base and represents an acknowledgment that prospects for volume growth, at least short term, remain lackluster. After full-year 2024 results, the bank lowers 2025 EBIT estimates by 1% to reflect the lackluster volume growth expectations in the express business in a context of increased price competition from peers. UBS lifts its price target to 42.50 euros from 37 euros and keeps its neutral rating. Shares fall 0.4% to 41.63 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1417 GMT - German auto manufacturer Volkswagen is well-positioned to handle challenges in the auto sector such as tariff threats, making it possible for its bonds to deliver excess returns, CreditSights analysts say in a note. Volkswagen's recent labor agreement could enable it to lower its costs and the company is expected to benefit significantly from Europe's regulatory changes on emission standards, CreditSights says. The automaker has good geographic diversification, a strong balance sheet, resilient free cash flow, and great equity value, they say. "We still believe that Volkswagen is the most compelling place for investors to generate excess returns in the investment-grade auto's space." (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

1408 GMT - Volkswagen results confirm an inflection point, Deutsche Bank analyst Tim Rokossa writes. Fourth-quarter earnings were 19% ahead of consensus and the 2025 guidance calls for mid-single-digit upgrades, he says. "There could be further upside from a potential Co2 target change in Europe, cost cutting and model momentum." The bank believes the stock is still very much under-owned and sees the story continuing to work. The bank lifts its target price to 125 euros from 115 euros and retains its buy rating. Shares fall 0.5% to 110.70 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1405 GMT - Kuehne + Nagel is well run but has limited growth, Jefferies analyst Michael Aspinall writes. On an organic growth basis, Kuehne + Nagel and rival DSV are equally matched, which makes capital allocation the differentiator, he says. "On this basis, an in-line valuation versus DSV appears stretched for lower growth." Kuehne + Nagel is more likely to seek acquisitions near term. However, this may be limited to bolt-ons, Jefferies says. The bank prefers exposure to air transport over sea in 2025. With the largest exposure to sea freight and few inorganic growth catalysts, the bank starts the company at hold with a 240 Swiss franc price target. Shares fall 1.7% to 207.30 Swiss francs. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1352 GMT - Deutsche Post's express business is a prized asset that has seen capacity grow ahead of demand, Jefferies analyst Michael Aspinall writes. This has led to cost inefficiencies and lower margins, but as demand improves the bank expects strong positive operating leverage and in the meantime management are addressing costs. "The valuation is more than underwritten by express but eCommerce is growing and supply chain can soak up excess capital at a circa 15% return on invested capital." The stock is the bank's top pick in transport. Jefferies starts Deutsche Post at buy with a 60 euros price target. Shares fall 0.5% to 41.58 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1337 GMT - Volkswagen's lack of free cash flow generation versus peers can justify some of the recent pessimism, but Citi says investors have overextrapolated recent challenges. The bank expects Volkswagen's free cash flow will improve as previous strategic errors have been amended, while European and German sentiment continues to improve. On the other hand, Chinese competition and electric vehicle challenges will continue. "Considering some investors thought last year that VW would find it difficult to stay afloat for next 5 years - the full-year 2024 results show scant evidence of this." VW has a plan. Costs are being cut, wage growth will slow, and German VW brand capacity has been cut. 2024 results were solid while 2025 guide is mixed. Shares fall 0.8%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

1128 GMT - Porsche still faces competitive challenges, leading the company to expect another transition year in 2025, Morgan Stanley analysts Javier Martinez de Olcoz Cerdan and Shaqeal A Kirunda write. The company sees 2025 as a year to undertake extensive measures to improve profitability, through the expansion of the product pipeline and improving availability of customization. Porsche will also adjust the corporate organization. Porsche has managed to gradually reduce its vehicle sales exposure to China to 16%, from around 32% in 2022, the bank says. "Investors have long been concerned about Porsche in China, and volumes may soon approach a lower bound at which this is no longer relevant." Fourth-quarter results were in line with lowered expectations following February's profit warning. Shares fall 4.4%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: Hang Seng Down 0.6% at Finish
data.description:
The Hang Seng shed 138 points or 0.6% to close at 23,463 on Thursday, extending losses for a second session amid broad-based declines.

The tech index fell 1.5%, dragged by Semicon Manufacturing (-4.9%), Meituan (-2.2%), and Alibaba Hlds. (-2.5%).

Sentiment weakened as the Trump administration escalated trade barriers, prompting retaliations from the EU and Canada.

China also vowed to take all necessary measures to safeguard its interests.

Consumer and property stocks dipped sharply, on doubts over China’s ability to meet its 5% GDP growth target for 2025, unchanged from 2024, despite deflation risks and trade uncertainties.

On the corporate front, Budweiser APAC plans further job cuts, mainly in China, to reduce costs.

Limiting losses, U.S. CPI data for February came in below expectations, strengthening the case for Fed rate cuts.

Among worst performers were Tongcheng Travel (-8.0%), Cathay Pacific Airways (-5.3%), Horizon Robotics (-5.2%), Prada Spa (-3.5%), and Anta Sports (-3.4%).

data.title: Minmetals Development to Redeem Bonds in March
data.description:
Minmetals Development said its 2023 perpetual corporate bonds will be redeemed on March 24, with bond delisting also scheduled on the same day.

Investors must hold bonds by March 21 to receive the final principal and 4.7% interest payout, according to a Thursday filing with the Shanghai bourse.

data.title: Canadian Pacific Kansas City Subsidiary Issuing $1.2 Billion in Notes
data.description:
Canadian Pacific Kansas City said late Wednesday its Canadian Pacific Railway subsidiary will issue $600 million of 4.80% notes due 2030 and $600 million of 5.20% notes due 2035, guaranteed by the parent company.

The proceeds of the offering will primarily be used to refinance existing debt and for general corporate purposes, the company said.

data.title: Walker & Dunlop Arranges $176 Million Credit Facility for Rental Portfolio in Atlanta
data.description:
Walker & Dunlop said Wednesday that it arranged a $176 million credit facility for seven single-family rental communities in various neighborhoods within Atlanta, Georgia.

The portfolio consists of 709 homes totaling 1.4 million rentable square feet across the seven communities.

data.title: NOVONIX Receives City, County Approvals for $4.6 Million Acquisition of Tennessee of Battery Material Production Site
data.description:
NOVONIX said Wednesday it has received approvals from the city of Chattanooga and Hamilton County in Tennessee for the $4.6 million purchase of a 182-acre piece of land for a 31,500 metric ton synthetic graphite mass production plant, its second in the city.

The industrial-scale battery company also said it has received an estimated $54 million in tax and other benefits over a 15-year period.

The deals are subject to final approval by the Chattanooga Industrial Development Board and other conditions, the company said.

data.title: Simply Contact Expands European Reach with Strategic Growth in the UK and CEE
data.description:
LONDON, March 13, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — As businesses navigate shifting economic conditions and increasing customer expectations, the demand for high-quality outsourcing solutions continues to grow. Companies seek partners that can not only optimise costs but also enhance customer experience, drive efficiency, and provide scalable support.

Simply Contact, a leading European customer service outsourcing provider, is accelerating its expansion with strategic moves in both new and established markets. The company has entered the UK, strengthened its leadership team, and deepened its presence in Central and Eastern Europe (CEE) with a new office in Bucharest. Each step supports the company’s mission to deliver high-quality, scalable customer support solutions to meet evolving business needs.

Entering the UK marks a major milestone for Simply Contact. As a new member of The UK Contact Centre Forum, the company is now part of a premier network of industry professionals, strengthening its ability to engage with key decision-makers. To drive success in the region, Simply Contact has appointed John Cole as Regional Head of CX Client Solutions for the UK and Ireland.

With over 17 years of experience in business development, BPO, and customer experience, John has led revenue growth and operational improvements at various customer service agencies. His expertise spans logistics, finance, retail, and technology, giving him a broad understanding of what businesses need to deliver outstanding customer experiences.

Simply Contact has also reinforced its leadership team by appointing Anna Bielikova as Chief Operating Officer. With a background in contact centre operations, she has vast experience managing complex client relationships and company-wide processes. Now, at Simply Contact, she will oversee operational strategy and ensure the company’s services continue to scale effectively.

Simply Contact’s expansion is not just about entering new markets, but also about solidifying its position in regions where it already has a strong presence. The company has opened a new office in Bucharest and is actively expanding its team in Romania, reinforcing its capabilities in a well-established outsourcing hub. This investment enhances Simply Contact’s ability to provide multilingual support and flexible, cost-effective customer service solutions to European businesses.

With its UK market entry, new leadership, and a more substantial presence in CEE, Simply Contact is positioning itself for long-term growth. These strategic moves reflect a clear commitment to providing businesses with the expertise and infrastructure needed to deliver exceptional customer experiences at scale.

About Simply Contact

Founded in 2013, Simply Contact specialises in customer service outsourcing, offering multilingual and multichannel support to businesses worldwide. With six countries of presence (Poland, the UK, Ukraine, Romania, Bulgaria, and Moldova), the company is continually growing its reach to new locations.

Trusted by leading global brands, Simply Contact leverages over 11 years of expertise and 600+ skilled agents to handle more than 10 million customer requests annually.
